title: GEORGE: Ten NFL Thoughts From Super Bowl Weekend | PatsFans.com
description:
Some final thoughts coming off Super Bowl Weekend: 1.
Don’t listen to those scribes and TV personalities who say “The Patriots didn’t look like they belonged” or words similar.
I underestimated the entire Seattle team.
Count me in as liking the prospect of an 18-game schedule and the Super Bowl moving to President’s Day weekend.
Tags: AFC East Boston Red Sox Drake Maye free agency Jerod Mayo Josh Allen K'Lavon Chaisson Mike Vrabel New England Patriots NFL Draft NFL schedule Robert Spillane Seattle Seahawks Super Bowl Will Campbell
text:
Some final thoughts coming off Super Bowl Weekend:

1. Don’t listen to those scribes and TV personalities who say “The Patriots didn’t look like they belonged” or words similar. They are playing the overdramatic card, and it simply isn’t true. It’s more shock factor than it is fact. Of course, the Patriots belonged. How well would Denver have fared against Seattle? Buffalo? Houston? The Patriots won all the games they had to in order to make it to the Big Show. And then they ran into a hot team that played like a buzzsaw. Fact is, this writer did not figure that the Seattle defense would play as well as it did. The Patriots did belong; they didn’t win, but eventually you all will live with that fact. I actually am over Super Bowl XLII. Really.

2. I will leave it to the Patriot coaches and personnel department to figure out what to do about Will Campbell. Every media member who offered an opinion said that he needs to be moved to guard. Mike Vrabel said he’s staying at left tackle. The Patriots pick at 31, do they go left tackle again? What about right tackle? Morgan Moses is quite long in the tooth. Campbell is way too good a teammate and an eager youngster to treat harshly in this offseason. If he just needs to develop his technique, he will. If they really believe that his arms are too short, they’ll address it.

3. Geek of the week: This is out of left field. I’m giving it to me. I underestimated the entire Seattle team. Sam Darnold did not see ghosts. Kenneth Walker III bludgeoned the Patriots and earned a well-deserved MVP, when all I saw was nothing better than a college halfback who could score four touchdowns against a not-yet-championship Michigan team but would wilt in the NFL. You could pay me a million dollars, and I wouldn’t be able to tell you any of the starting 11 Seahawks defenders. Not that I didn’t think Seattle was good. I just didn’t see “championship good” in them. I do now. When I am wrong, I say I’m wrong.

4. I hope Robert Spillane and K’Lavon Chaisson re-sign with the Patriots. Don’t cheap out on these guys. These guys need to remain an integral part of the Patriot defense. The Patriots did a whale of a good job in holding Seattle to four field goals before the game got out of hand, they did a terrific job in containing Jaxon Smith-Njigba, and they helped the Patriots get to this game with some stellar work in the subordinate rounds. Spillane and Chaisson were integral parts of the defense. Tough choices need to be made, but unlike the Red Sox, the Patriots have to be very careful as to who they choose to keep and who they choose to cast loose.

5. Nice to see Vrabel get Coach of the Year. Yes, Drake Maye lost out on MVP. Yes, Bob Kraft and Bill Belichick lost out on Canton. It’s nice that the voters got this one right, and so did the Patriots. Kraft gave Jerod Mayo his one promised season as head coach, then smartly let him go in favor of Vrabel. There isn’t anyone who can say a bad thing about this guy. Vrabel has a chance to launch a nice run of Patriot excellence which could very well yield a Vince (or more) down the road. That said, let’s hope that Mayo doesn’t have to battle the Rooney Rule in his endeavours to become a head coach once again someday.

6. Back to school: Sorry, Tobacco Road, but the best college hoop games are in the Big Ten. Mostly it’s in the original ten teams and not so much in the new teams added in the last several years. The games are compelling and loaded with rabid and incredibly loud home fans. Yes, North Carolina versus Duke is always must see; wonder how much this game affects Drake and Ann Michael (lots, I’m sure) and how little it affects Jordon Hudson and her plus one (not at all, I’m betting). By the way, good for those schools’ football teams doing as well as they have over the last few years (well, the Tar Heels had been doing well, until…).

7. What will the AFC East look like a few years from now, with three of the four teams all breaking in new head coaches this year? Will we return to the tomato can years and just give the Patriots the division title in September just to keep things uncomplicated? Maybe not, but the Patriots will at least be favored to win the division for the foreseeable future. As long as Buffalo has Josh Allen, they have to be at least considered a contender. Miami will be interesting if they hand the offensive car keys to Quinn Ewers. The Jets will still be the Jets, they have so many needs and so much culture to build that not even an AI Vince Lombardi could help them one iota.

8. Count me in as liking the prospect of an 18-game schedule and the Super Bowl moving to President’s Day weekend. They are including a second bye week in this proposal. The CFL plays 18 games. The old USFL also played 18 games. I don’t root for commissioner Roger Goodell very often, but I’m with him on this subject. Get it off the odd number of games; I am shocked that no team has ever complained when they have 8 home games and 9 road games. Then go back to the old playoff format of two bye teams per conference during Wild Card weekend. I for one would like to have seen the Patriots get a bye as the two seed this year.

9. Do not, repeat, do not do anything with the TV packages that reduce the number of fans to be able to watch games on these various streaming networks. We got way too comfy with Prime Video now being a mainstream pro football channel. Now Netflix is starting to get its meat hooks into the broadcasting action. Many more could be coming as well. It was bad enough when DirecTV lost NFL Sunday Ticket to YouTube TV. Since switching over to YTTV, all the NBC Sports Boston I get to see are 14-minute segments on YouTube (the original one). I rue the day when I have to go bootleg to watch a Patriots game. May that day never happen.

10. Finally, a shoutout to all non-football-related issues everywhere. Someone tell John Henry to get back to spending to make the Red Sox competitive or sell the team. Jayson Tatum continues to behave poorly as he rehabs from his Achilles injury. Sit him down and explain to him that the Celtics need him to win their next title, and to grow the hell up. If you are jealous of the success the Dodgers have had, don’t bring up Mookie Betts in your rant. Betts had no desire to remain in Boston, and they had to trade him. Also, easy does it when you bring up that of the four players acquired for Rafael Devers, three have been traded away. Devers shot his way out of town, and there wasn’t much the Sox could do. Get UMass football the hell out of FCS. I was all for tha,t and I was wrong. Make Lindsey Vonn listen to her dad and retire. She has nothing more to prove and she is one of the all-time great Olympians. The Winter Games look better in small towns like Cortina and not in big cities like Beijing.

Spring training is now on. Can’t wait for Garrett Crochet to throw the first competitive pitch of the 2026 Red Sox season. Take care, all, it will be late July before you know it.

We invite you to catch Bob George’s Boston Sports Podcast, broadcast on YouTube. Go to YouTube handle thepic413 to view the podcasts. Please click on the Subscribe button so you don’t miss any of the podcasts.

Tags: AFC East Boston Red Sox Drake Maye free agency Jerod Mayo Josh Allen K'Lavon Chaisson Mike Vrabel New England Patriots NFL Draft NFL schedule Robert Spillane Seattle Seahawks Super Bowl Will Campbell

title: Ranking Colts' top unrestricted free agents: How much to retain Alec Pierce? - The Athletic
description:
To deliver on that claim, here are Indy’s top five unrestricted free agents, ranked in order of who I think Irsay-Gordon and general manager Chris Ballard should prioritize retaining.
QB Daniel Jones Jones probably isn’t someone a franchise should depend on, considering his lengthy injury history, which now includes a torn right Achilles that ended his promising 2025 season.
WR Alec Pierce From a team standpoint, Jones is the most important free agent to re-sign because the only other QBs the Colts have on their roster are oft-injured 2023 No.
However, from a league-wide perspective, Pierce is easily the Colts’ top free agent.
The Colts may consider using the franchise tag on Pierce to ensure the 25-year-old can’t leave in free agency.
text:
Shortly after the Seattle Seahawks defeated the New England Patriots in the Super Bowl, Seahawks quarterback Sam Darnold stood on a stage at Levi’s Stadium holding the Lombardi Trophy. The 2018 No. 3 pick, now with his fifth team, had just completed one of the greatest redemption stories in NFL history by leading the Seahawks to their first Super Bowl title in 12 years.

“You go through tough times for a reason,” Darnold said a day later on ESPN. “I’ve had great people in my corner to be able to lift me up.”

Darnold was the most impactful free-agent signing in the NFL last year, and in just over a month, every team will once again be searching for upgrades when the 2026 free agency begins on March 11.

The Indianapolis Colts are in the midst of a five-year playoff drought, but the “sense of urgency” to get back in the postseason has never been higher, according to team owner Carlie Irsay-Gordon. To deliver on that claim, here are Indy’s top five unrestricted free agents, ranked in order of who I think Irsay-Gordon and general manager Chris Ballard should prioritize retaining.

1. QB Daniel Jones

Jones probably isn’t someone a franchise should depend on, considering his lengthy injury history, which now includes a torn right Achilles that ended his promising 2025 season.

However, the Colts don’t have any other options in 2026. They essentially tied their future to Jones in November when Ballard traded their 2026 and 2027 first-round picks, as well as wide receiver AD Mitchell, to the New York Jets for star cornerback Sauce Gardner. That massive trade likely would not have happened if the Colts were iffy about Jones, though they also couldn’t have predicted that Jones would be lost for the season just five weeks later.

Despite his Achilles injury, all signs point to Jones being retained in Indianapolis. In Ballard’s end-of-season news conference, he stamped Jones as the team’s short- and long-term answer at QB. Now, it’s likely a matter of what Jones’ new contract will look like, given that he’s coming off arguably the best season of his career but also a devastating injury.

Jones threw 19 touchdown passes against eight interceptions and had five rushing TDs, while playing on a one-year, $14 million prove-it deal. He was likely on par for a payday similar to the three-year, $100.5 million deal Darnold signed with the Seahawks last year, but Jones’ potential annual salary will likely take a dip because of the injury. How much it drops remains to be seen, though it feels like a formality that he’ll be back in a Colts jersey.

2. WR Alec Pierce

From a team standpoint, Jones is the most important free agent to re-sign because the only other QBs the Colts have on their roster are oft-injured 2023 No. 4 pick Anthony Richardson and 2025 sixth-round pick Riley Leonard. However, from a league-wide perspective, Pierce is easily the Colts’ top free agent.

The 2022 second-round pick has improved each season of his career, and he supplanted veteran Michael Pittman Jr. as the team’s No. 1 receiver in 2025. Pierce led the league in yards per reception (21.3) for the second straight season and eclipsed 1,000 receiving yards for the first time in his career. His ability to flip the field on deep catches and win 50/50 balls, while also expanding his route tree to have more of an impact in the short and intermediate passing game, will have suitors lining up to sign him.

Pierce is projected to earn $20.2 million annually on his next contract, per Spotrac, though it wouldn’t be surprising if that payday is higher. The Colts may consider using the franchise tag on Pierce to ensure the 25-year-old can’t leave in free agency. The 2026 wide receiver franchise tag is estimated to be a fully guaranteed $28.8 million, per Over the Cap, and would also allow the team to negotiate a long-term deal.

3. SS Nick Cross

After a rocky start to his NFL career, Cross proved to be an impactful starter for the second straight season. He finished with five pass breakups and just one interception but showed off his versatility with career highs of 2 1/2 sacks and six QB hits in 2025. Colts defensive coordinator Lou Anarumo sometimes used Cross as a hybrid linebacker, allowing him to play closer to the line of scrimmage. Yet, Cross is still fast and agile enough not get picked on when he drops in coverage.

The Colts would probably like to retain Cross, whom they traded up to draft in the third round in 2022, but they’ll have to decide if they’re willing to put even more money into an already expensive secondary. Free safety Cam Bynum, as well as cornerbacks Gardner and Charvarius Ward Sr., are all on lucrative contracts. Ward is contemplating retirement because of concussions, but if he returns, the Colts may want to use the money they’d spend on Cross to upgrade elsewhere, most notably on the underwhelming defensive line.

Cross is projected to make $6.1 million annually on his next contract, per Spotrac. However, the safety market has been finicky, with the value of the position declining in recent years. If Cross walks in free agency, Indy would likely need to find a cheaper replacement in the draft or bet on an unproven player like 2025 seventh-round pick Hunter Wohler, who showed promise in training camp but missed his entire rookie season due to a Lisfranc injury.

4. DT Neville Gallimore

Gallimore isn’t a starter like the aforementioned players on this list, but he was arguably the Colts’ best bang-for-your-buck player in 2025.

The veteran defensive tackle signed a one-year, $1.3 million deal to join Indianapolis last year, and he delivered his best season with career highs of 3 1/2 sacks and four tackles for loss while playing just 40 percent of Indy’s defensive snaps.

Gallimore has earned a bump in salary, with Spotrac projecting that he’ll net $2.2 million annually on his next contract, and if that’s all it takes to bring him back, then it should be a no-brainer.

5. TE Mo Alie-Cox

Alie-Cox isn’t going to blow anyone away with his stats, but that’s not why the 2017 undrafted free agent has survived in the NFL this long. He’s stuck around because of the dirty work, and Alie-Cox was clearly one of the better blocking tight ends in the NFL again in 2025.

His role in the trenches can’t be overlooked, particularly in the running game, and his skill set pairs well with the more pass-catching-oriented Tyler Warren, who had a stellar rookie campaign.

The running joke among the local media is that Ballard would never get rid of Alie-Cox, and for as much as Indy’s GM deserves to be criticized, keeping Alie-Cox around would not be a mistake.

The 32-year-old is projected to make $2.2 million annually on his next contract, per Spotrac, which is just a drop in the bucket when considering the team’s other, more expensive and pressing needs.

Final thought

There were a few other free agents to consider for this list, including right tackle Braden Smith, defensive end Kwity Paye and linebacker Germaine Pratt. Of those three, Pratt seems like he’d be the most likely to return because he was on a minimum deal and has played for several seasons under Anarumo.

However, I’m curious to see how much the Colts revamp their linebacker corps, given how much Pratt and veteran Zaire Franklin struggled in coverage. Franklin is under contract through 2027, but the Colts could release him this offseason and create $5.8 million in cap space while carrying just $2.5 million in dead money.

Unlike Pratt, Smith and Paye don’t have much of an argument for retention.

Paye, the Colts’ 2021 first-round pick, had just four sacks in 17 games in 2025. His five-year tenure has been disappointing, and Indianapolis has no incentive to bring him back if it plans to upgrade on the edge.

Meanwhile, Smith ended the season on injured reserve because of a concussion and neck injury, and the Colts appear to have already drafted his replacement in either 2024 third-round pick Matt Goncalves or 2025 fourth-round pick Jalen Travis.

text:
The Eagles won 11 games and earned a second straight NFC East crown in 2025 … but it was still a disappointing season.

Coming off a Super Bowl LIX victory there was a real hope that the Eagles would at least be able to come close to a repeat and that didn’t happen. Their offense sputtered for much of the season and the Eagles lost at home to the banged-up 49ers in the wild-card round.

Now it’s time to turn our attention to 2026.

The Eagles should look different next season with a new offensive coordinator and new offensive staff. We’ll also see how different the roster looks next season; there will definitely be some changes.

The Eagles are tied for the fourth-shortest odds to win Super Bowl LXI next year in Los Angeles. Here’s a look at the top 10 from Draft Kings:

Seahawks: +950

Rams: +950

Bills: +1100

Eagles: +1300

Patriots: +1300

Ravens: +1300

Packers: +1400

Lions: +1400

Chargers: +1500

Chiefs: +1500

As we continue to look ahead to the 2026 season, here’s a roundup of where the Eagles rank on way-too-early power rankings for next season:

NFL

Fox Sports: 5

Sporting News: 6

ESPN: 8

Bleacher Report: 9

These four lists all have the Eagles in the top 10 but the highest they get is No. 5. Their average spot based on these rankings is No. 7.

Their highest ranking comes from Fox Sports’ Ralph Vacchiano, who writes: “Well, they are on an every-other-year plan with the Super Bowl. They’re also still talented enough to be the class of their division.”

The only teams ahead of the Eagles on the Fox Sports’ list are the Patriots at 4, Seahawks at 3, Rams at 2 and Broncos at 1.

Some teams from the other lists who were ranked ahead of the Eagles: Lions, 49ers, Bills, Chiefs, Bears, Jaguars, Broncos.
